exp_L1_00.label: Diagnostic Accuracy & Trust
exp_L1_00.description: Concerns about whether AI outputs are reliable enough to inform real clinical decisions

exp_L2_00_00.label: False Positives/Negatives
exp_L2_00_00.description: AI misses things it should catch or flags normal findings as abnormal

exp_leaf_00_00_00.label: Misses subtle early cancers on mammograms
exp_leaf_00_00_00.description: The AI says 'negative,' but I spot a tiny spiculated nodule the algorithm glossed over — especially in dense breasts where contrast is low.

exp_leaf_00_00_01.label: Overcalls benign lung nodules as suspicious
exp_leaf_00_00_01.description: It keeps flagging stable, calcified granulomas like they’re growing tumors — makes me waste time chasing ghosts.

exp_leaf_00_00_02.label: Struggles with rare pathologies
exp_leaf_00_00_02.description: If it hasn’t seen 500 examples of lymphangitic carcinomatosis, it just shrugs — and labels it 'unremarkable interstitium.'

exp_leaf_00_00_03.label: Inconsistent performance across vendors
exp_leaf_00_00_03.description: One AI tool nails pneumothorax detection; another misses half of them. There’s no standard benchmark we can actually trust.

exp_L2_00_01.label: Lack of Explainability
exp_L2_00_01.description: AI gives an answer but no clear reason why — making it hard to validate or learn from

exp_leaf_00_01_00.label: No visual heatmap or saliency map for critical findings
exp_leaf_00_01_00.description: It says 'positive for fracture' but doesn’t show me where — so I have to hunt through the whole image anyway.

exp_leaf_00_01_01.label: Can’t tell if it’s using real pathology or imaging artifact
exp_leaf_00_01_01.description: Sometimes it’s reacting to motion blur or metal streaking, not actual disease — but there’s no way to know without reverse-engineering it.

exp_leaf_00_01_02.label: Feels like guessing without clinical context
exp_leaf_00_01_02.description: It doesn’t know the patient has a history of TB or recent surgery — so it interprets post-op changes as new infection.

exp_leaf_00_01_03.label: Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling
exp_leaf_00_01_03.description: I see something off — maybe texture, maybe asymmetry — but the AI says 'clear.' Without explanation, I’m stuck doubting myself.

exp_L2_00_02.label: Calibration & Confidence
exp_L2_00_02.description: AI confidence scores don’t match real-world reliability

exp_leaf_00_02_00.label: High-confidence errors that look obvious in hindsight
exp_leaf_00_02_00.description: It says '99% sure it’s benign,' but it’s clearly a melanoma metastasis — how is that possible?

exp_leaf_00_02_01.label: Low-confidence on easy cases
exp_leaf_00_02_01.description: A textbook pneumothorax gets a 52% probability — like it’s unsure whether air exists outside the lung.

exp_leaf_00_02_02.label: Confidence thresholds feel arbitrary
exp_leaf_00_02_02.description: Who decided 85% is 'actionable'? That number shifts between studies and never matches our local prevalence.

exp_L1_01.label: Workflow Integration
exp_L1_01.description: How AI tools fit (or don’t fit) into existing clinical routines and systems

exp_L2_01_00.label: Alert Fatigue & Interruptions
exp_L2_01_00.description: Too many pop-ups, banners, or second-guesses that slow me down

exp_leaf_01_00_00.label: Constant pop-ups during emergency reads
exp_leaf_01_00_00.description: In trauma bay, I’m trying to find free air or bleeds, and the AI keeps interrupting with 'possible incidentaloma' alerts — zero relevance.

exp_leaf_01_00_01.label: Forced acknowledgment of every AI suggestion
exp_leaf_01_00_01.description: I have to click 'agree/disagree' on 30 findings per chest X-ray — even when they’re obviously wrong. It’s like digital paperwork.

exp_leaf_01_00_02.label: Can’t silence low-yield alerts selectively
exp_leaf_01_00_02.description: I’d love to mute 'possible atelectasis' flags on post-op patients, but the system treats all alerts the same.

exp_leaf_01_00_03.label: Disrupts natural reading sequence
exp_leaf_01_00_03.description: I read head-to-toe systematically, but the AI highlights pelvis first — throws off my mental flow and I might miss something upstream.

exp_L2_01_01.label: PACS & EHR Compatibility
exp_L2_01_01.description: AI tools don’t play nice with our existing imaging and record systems

exp_leaf_01_01_00.label: AI overlays don’t render in legacy PACS
exp_leaf_01_01_00.description: We upgraded one workstation, but half the department still uses old GE Centricity — and the heatmaps just vanish.

exp_leaf_01_01_01.label: Findings don’t auto-populate structured reports
exp_leaf_01_01_01.description: The AI detects a 12mm nodule, but I still have to type it manually into the template — defeats the purpose.

exp_leaf_01_01_02.label: No audit trail linking AI input to final report
exp_leaf_01_01_02.description: If there’s a lawsuit, I can’t prove whether I saw the AI flag or overruled it — the system doesn’t log that interaction.

exp_L2_01_02.label: Time Pressure & Efficiency
exp_L2_01_02.description: AI promises speed but often adds steps or delays

exp_leaf_01_02_00.label: Adds 30 seconds per study waiting for inference
exp_leaf_01_02_00.description: On a 200-study day, that’s an extra hour just watching spinning wheels — and it crashes half the time.

exp_leaf_01_02_01.label: Double-checking AI takes longer than reading alone
exp_leaf_01_02_01.description: Now I have to verify both what I see AND what the AI claims — it’s like proofreading someone else’s messy draft.

exp_leaf_01_02_02.label: Slows down trainees who rely too much on it
exp_leaf_01_02_02.description: Residents wait for the AI to 'tell them what’s there' instead of learning to look — their pattern recognition skills atrophy.

exp_L1_02.label: Regulatory, Legal & Liability
exp_L1_02.description: Uncertainty around who’s responsible when AI contributes to a diagnostic error

exp_L2_02_00.label: Accountability Gaps
exp_L2_02_00.description: Blurred lines between human and machine responsibility

exp_leaf_02_00_00.label: Am I liable if I ignore a correct AI alert?
exp_leaf_02_00_00.description: AI flagged a small bleed I dismissed as artifact — turns out it was real. Did I commit negligence by overriding it?

exp_leaf_02_00_01.label: Am I liable if I trust a wrong AI finding?
exp_leaf_02_00_01.description: I reported a 'fracture' the AI highlighted — but it was just a vessel. Now the patient got unnecessary surgery. Who pays?

exp_leaf_02_00_02.label: Vendor disclaims all clinical responsibility
exp_leaf_02_00_02.description: Their FDA clearance says 'decision support only' — so if it fails, I’m holding the bag alone.

exp_L2_02_01.label: Regulatory Uncertainty
exp_L2_02_01.description: Evolving rules make it hard to adopt or validate tools confidently

exp_leaf_02_01_00.label: FDA-cleared ≠ clinically validated
exp_leaf_02_01_00.description: Just because it passed regulatory muster doesn’t mean it works in *my* hospital with *my* scanners and *my* patient mix.

exp_leaf_02_01_01.label: No clear pathway for model updates
exp_leaf_02_01_01.description: If the vendor tweaks the algorithm next month, do I need IRB approval again? Recertify? Nobody knows.

exp_leaf_02_01_02.label: Hospital legal won’t approve anything without indemnification
exp_leaf_02_01_02.description: Our risk management team blocks every AI pilot unless the vendor signs a hold-harmless clause — which none will do.

exp_L2_02_02.label: Audit & Documentation Burden
exp_L2_02_02.description: New requirements to track, justify, and store AI interactions

exp_leaf_02_02_00.label: Must document every AI override in dictation
exp_leaf_02_02_00.description: Now I have to say 'AI suggested X, but I believe Y because...' for every discrepancy — adds minutes per case.

exp_leaf_02_02_01.label: No standard for storing AI metadata with images
exp_leaf_02_02_01.description: DICOM doesn’t handle model version, confidence score, or input preprocessing — so future reviewers won’t know what the AI actually saw.

exp_L1_03.label: Data Quality & Generalizability
exp_L1_03.description: AI trained on ideal data fails on real-world, messy clinical inputs

exp_L2_03_00.label: Scanner & Protocol Variability
exp_L2_03_00.description: Performance tanks when imaging hardware or settings differ from training data

exp_leaf_03_00_00.label: Fails on low-dose or motion-degraded scans
exp_leaf_03_00_00.description: In ER or pediatrics, we use fast, noisy protocols — and the AI trained on pristine academic scans just gives up.

exp_leaf_03_00_01.label: Vendor-specific bias (Siemens vs GE vs Philips)
exp_leaf_03_00_01.description: Works great on Siemens but hallucinates on GE because of different reconstruction kernels — feels like it’s tuned for one brand.

exp_leaf_03_00_02.label: Protocol mismatch (e.g., non-contrast vs contrast)
exp_leaf_03_00_02.description: It expects contrast-enhanced liver MRI but gets non-contrast — then calls every vessel a mass.

exp_L2_03_01.label: Population & Disease Spectrum Bias
exp_L2_03_01.description: Models trained on narrow demographics underperform elsewhere

exp_leaf_03_01_00.label: Poor performance in diverse or underserved populations
exp_leaf_03_01_00.description: Trained mostly on white, insured patients — so it misses TB patterns common in immigrant communities or sarcoid in Black patients.

exp_leaf_03_01_01.label: Doesn’t handle comorbidities well
exp_leaf_03_01_01.description: Patient has CHF, COPD, and cancer — the AI tries to explain everything as one thing and misses the rest.

exp_leaf_03_01_02.label: Struggles with post-treatment anatomy
exp_leaf_03_01_02.description: After surgery or radiation, normal anatomy looks weird — and the AI screams 'recurrence!' every time.

exp_L2_03_02.label: Label Noise & Ground Truth Issues
exp_L2_03_02.description: Training data labels are often imperfect or inconsistent

exp_leaf_03_02_00.label: Trained on radiology reports, not expert consensus
exp_leaf_03_02_00.description: Uses dictated reports full of hedging language ('concerning for...') as truth — so it learns uncertainty as certainty.

exp_leaf_03_02_01.label: No distinction between incidental vs clinically significant
exp_leaf_03_02_01.description: Labels a 3mm lung nodule the same as a 3cm mass — but clinically, we ignore the former.

exp_L1_04.label: Professional Identity & Team Dynamics
exp_L1_04.description: How AI reshapes roles, relationships, and expertise in radiology

exp_L2_04_00.label: Erosion of Diagnostic Authority
exp_L2_04_00.description: Referring physicians or administrators question radiologist judgment when AI disagrees

exp_leaf_04_00_00.label: Clinicians demand AI concordance before accepting my read
exp_leaf_04_00_00.description: Surgeons now say, 'But the AI said no fracture!' — as if my 10 years of training count less than an algorithm.

exp_leaf_04_00_01.label: Administrators use AI accuracy stats to justify staffing cuts
exp_leaf_04_00_01.description: They point to '95% AI sensitivity' and say we don’t need as many radiologists — ignoring all the nuance we provide.

exp_leaf_04_00_02.label: Pressure to conform to AI output even when wrong
exp_leaf_04_00_02.description: There’s unspoken expectation to align with the AI to avoid 'discrepancy flags' in QA — so I sometimes bite my tongue.

exp_L2_04_01.label: Training & Skill Degradation
exp_L2_04_01.description: Concerns that AI reliance harms education and long-term expertise

exp_leaf_04_01_00.label: Trainees skip foundational learning
exp_leaf_04_01_00.description: Why memorize bone landmarks when AI labels everything? But then they can’t interpret when the AI fails.

exp_leaf_04_01_01.label: Fewer opportunities to learn from mistakes
exp_leaf_04_01_01.description: AI catches obvious errors early — so residents never experience the 'aha!' moment of missing something and correcting it later.

exp_leaf_04_01_02.label: No curriculum for AI critique or validation
exp_leaf_04_01_02.description: We teach anatomy and pathology, but not how to interrogate an AI’s weaknesses — so everyone treats it like gospel.

exp_L2_04_02.label: Interdisciplinary Tensions
exp_L2_04_02.description: Misalignment between radiologists, data scientists, and clinicians on AI expectations

exp_leaf_04_02_00.label: Data scientists don’t understand clinical urgency
exp_leaf_04_02_00.description: They optimize for AUC, not whether it helps me find a bleed in under 2 minutes during stroke call.

exp_leaf_04_02_01.label: Clinicians expect AI to replace radiology consults
exp_leaf_04_02_01.description: ICU teams want AI to 'just tell them the answer' without understanding the uncertainty or limitations.

exp_leaf_04_02_02.label: Radiologists excluded from AI design decisions
exp_leaf_04_02_02.description: Vendors build features based on sales demos, not real workflow pain — because they never shadowed a night float shift.

res_L1_00.label: Expert Mental Models of Discovery
res_L1_00.description: Understanding how domain experts conceptualize and narrate the process of discovering new cell states, including their definitions of 'discovery' and key decision points.

res_L2_00_00.label: Discovery Workflow Structure
res_L2_00_00.description: How experts decompose the end-to-end cell state discovery pipeline into discrete steps and transitions.

res_leaf_00_00_00.label: Map the canonical sequence of steps from raw data to confirmed cell state
res_leaf_00_00_00.description: Elicit a detailed, step-by-step account of the typical workflow, identifying where handoffs between computational and biological reasoning occur.

res_leaf_00_00_01.label: Identify the moment experts consider 'true discovery' to occur
res_leaf_00_00_01.description: Determine at which stage in the pipeline experts feel a novel biological insight has genuinely emerged (e.g., clustering output vs. validation).

res_leaf_00_00_02.label: Characterize confidence distribution across workflow stages
res_leaf_00_00_02.description: Understand which steps experts feel most/least confident in, and whether uncertainty is technical, statistical, or biological in nature.

res_L2_00_01.label: Tacit Knowledge and Heuristics
res_L2_00_01.description: Uncover informal rules, intuitions, and experience-based judgments that guide decisions but are rarely codified.

res_leaf_00_01_00.label: Elicit unformalizable 'experience rules' used during clustering or interpretation
res_leaf_00_01_00.description: Capture heuristics experts rely on that resist algorithmic encoding (e.g., 'this cluster looks messy, so it’s probably doublets').

res_leaf_00_01_01.label: Identify signals prioritized when evaluating a candidate cell state
res_leaf_00_01_01.description: Determine the relative importance experts assign to marker genes, spatial context, functional enrichment, etc., and how they trade off conflicting evidence.

res_leaf_00_01_02.label: Understand criteria for 'credible' cell state claims
res_leaf_00_01_02.description: Clarify what combination of computational, spatial, and wet-lab evidence constitutes sufficient proof for a new state.

res_L1_01.label: Decision-Making Under Uncertainty
res_L1_01.description: Exploring how experts navigate ambiguity, especially in clustering resolution and cluster interpretation.

res_L2_01_00.label: Clustering Resolution Choices
res_L2_01_00.description: How experts select and justify granularity levels in unsupervised clustering.

res_leaf_01_00_00.label: Understand strategies for choosing clustering resolution
res_leaf_01_00_00.description: Identify whether resolution is driven by biological priors, algorithmic metrics (e.g., silhouette score), or iterative exploration.

res_leaf_01_00_01.label: Determine triggers for suspecting a novel cell state
res_leaf_01_00_01.description: Elicit the specific patterns or anomalies that prompt experts to hypothesize a new state rather than noise or artifact.

res_L2_01_01.label: Iterative Refinement Practices
res_L2_01_01.description: How experts revise clusters when initial results seem biologically implausible.

res_leaf_01_01_00.label: Characterize cluster adjustment behaviors
res_leaf_01_01_00.description: Document whether and how experts manually merge, split, or discard clusters based on biological plausibility.

res_leaf_01_01_01.label: Identify sources of iteration friction
res_leaf_01_01_01.description: Pinpoint which parts of re-clustering or re-embedding are most time-consuming or cognitively taxing.

res_L1_02.label: Tool Use and Interaction Pain Points
res_L1_02.description: Assessing how current computational tools support or hinder the discovery process.

res_L2_02_00.label: Tool Ecosystem Landscape
res_L2_02_00.description: Mapping the software stack experts rely on and their division of labor across tools.

res_leaf_02_00_00.label: Inventory primary tools and custom scripts used in discovery
res_leaf_02_00_00.description: Catalog standard platforms (e.g., Seurat, Scanpy) versus bespoke code, and understand why certain tasks require custom solutions.

res_L2_02_01.label: Usability Gaps in Analysis Tools
res_L2_02_01.description: Identifying features that work well versus those that create bottlenecks.

res_leaf_02_01_00.label: Identify highly efficient tool features
res_leaf_02_01_00.description: Determine which functionalities experts find intuitive, fast, or well-integrated into their mental model.

res_leaf_02_01_01.label: Pinpoint unavoidable but burdensome tool interactions
res_leaf_02_01_01.description: Surface repetitive, error-prone, or unintuitive steps that experts tolerate due to lack of alternatives.

res_leaf_02_01_02.label: Assess support for hypothesis testing within tools
res_leaf_02_01_02.description: Evaluate whether current tools facilitate rapid testing of biological hypotheses (e.g., 'Is this cluster enriched for pathway X?').

res_L1_03.label: Interpretability Needs in Biological Context
res_L1_03.description: Understanding what kinds of explanations or visualizations would make AI-assisted diagnosis more trustworthy and actionable.

res_L2_03_00.label: Evidence Integration Challenges
res_L2_03_00.description: How experts synthesize diverse data modalities to form coherent biological narratives.

res_leaf_03_00_00.label: Map multi-modal evidence requirements for validation
res_leaf_03_00_00.description: Clarify how marker genes, spatial data, pathway enrichment, and external replication jointly contribute to confidence.

res_leaf_03_00_01.label: Identify gaps between computational output and biological meaning
res_leaf_03_00_01.description: Determine where algorithmic results fail to map cleanly onto biological concepts (e.g., continuous trajectories vs. discrete states).

res_L2_03_01.label: Barriers to Biological Interpretation
res_L2_03_01.description: What makes translating clusters into biological insights difficult.

res_leaf_03_01_00.label: Assess difficulty of biological sense-making post-clustering
res_leaf_03_01_00.description: Understand whether the hardest part is statistical (e.g., distinguishing signal from noise) or conceptual (e.g., naming a state).

res_leaf_03_01_01.label: Characterize challenges in communicating cell states to collaborators
res_leaf_03_01_01.description: Explore how experts justify or present tentative discoveries to wet-lab biologists or clinicians.

res_L1_04.label: Explainability Requirements for AI-Assisted Diagnosis
res_L1_04.description: Deriving design implications for XAI systems that support medical cell state discovery.

res_L2_04_00.label: Trust Calibration in AI Outputs
res_L2_04_00.description: How experts assess reliability of AI-generated clusters or annotations.

res_leaf_04_00_00.label: Define conditions under which an AI-suggested cell state is deemed trustworthy
res_leaf_04_00_00.description: Elicit the minimal set of explainable features (e.g., top markers, uncertainty scores) needed to accept an AI proposal.

res_L2_04_01.label: Support for Expert Judgment
res_L2_04_01.description: How AI tools can better augment—rather than replace—expert intuition.

res_leaf_04_01_00.label: Identify opportunities for AI to surface tacit heuristics explicitly
res_leaf_04_01_00.description: Explore whether AI could formalize or visualize expert rules (e.g., flagging clusters that violate known spatial constraints).

res_leaf_04_01_01.label: Determine needs for interactive hypothesis refinement
res_leaf_04_01_01.description: Assess demand for tools that let experts tweak cluster definitions and immediately see downstream biological implications.

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on ensuring AI diagnoses account for critical patient-specific clinical context—like prior TB or recent surgery—is essential for avoiding dangerous misinterpretations in real-world care.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on clinical history without grounding diagnostic confidence in multi-modal biological evidence can leave clinicians vulnerable to false reassurance when underlying disease mechanisms are misunderstood.
pivot.research_goal: Map multi-modal evidence requirements for validation—integrating genomic, spatial, and pathway data to build biologically coherent diagnostic narratives.
pivot.compelling_reason: This approach strengthens your clinical judgment by anchoring AI interpretations in verifiable biological reality, reducing guesswork even when patient history is incomplete or ambiguous.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical morning, a clinician reviews an AI-generated diagnosis that flags a suspicious lung finding. Instead of second-guessing whether it’s post-op change or infection, they see a unified view showing how imaging, gene activity, and tissue architecture all converge on a single explanation—giving them confidence to act decisively without waiting for more tests.

aligned_path_expert: Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling
aligned_path_researcher: Elicit unformalizable 'experience rules' used during clustering or interpretation

conflict.researcher_branch: Elicit unformalizable 'experience rules' used during clustering or interpretation

probes.question: Is your concern more related to overall visual pattern recognition or to specific localized asymmetries?
Which matters more when you feel something is off: subtle asymmetry detection or spatial texture irregularity?
When the AI says 'clear' but you disagree, is your judgment driven more by decision heuristics or by contextual anomaly salience?
Do you rely more on spatial texture irregularity or boundary sharpness perception when questioning AI output?
Is your gut feeling triggered more by contextual anomaly salience or by temporal consistency of visual cues?
When doubting the AI, do you weigh experience-based confidence thresholds more than decision heuristics?
Which influences your judgment more: temporal consistency of visual cues or spatial texture irregularity?
Is your concern primarily about boundary sharpness perception or subtle asymmetry detection?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the overall visual pattern appeared statistically normal but contained no recognizable biological structures, would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?

If the left and right sides of the structure were perfectly symmetrical in shape but differed only in grayscale intensity by 5%, would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?
If you had previously seen three similar cases that turned out to be false negatives from the AI, would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?

If the texture within the region of interest changed from granular to smooth while all other features remained identical, would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?
If the same visual finding appeared in an anatomically implausible location (e.g., lung tissue in a brain scan), would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?
If your years of experience increased from 5 to 20 while the image stayed exactly the same, would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?

If the current image matched prior scans from the same patient taken six months ago except for one new faint feature, would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?

If the edges of a suspicious region became slightly blurred (standard deviation of edge gradient reduced by 15%) while internal features stayed constant, would your judgment about 'Hard to reconcile AI output with my gut feeling' change?

Pinpoint unavoidable but burdensome tool interactions

conflict.expert_branch: Disrupts natural reading sequence
conflict.researcher_branch: Pinpoint unavoidable but burdensome tool interactions

analogy.source_concept: Forced acknowledgment of every AI suggestion

structural_mapping.inputs: AI-generated findings inserted into the diagnostic workflow
structural_mapping.logic: System mandates explicit user response regardless of relevance or confidence
structural_mapping.outputs: Cognitive overhead and workflow interruption from repetitive, low-value interactions

analogy.explanation: Just as forced acknowledgment imposes unavoidable clicks that fracture attention, the researcher's 'burdensome tool interactions' describe necessary but disruptive steps—like re-clustering—that interrupt diagnostic momentum even when the clinician knows they’re uninformative.

assistance_payload.scenario: During a trauma read, you expect to scan head-to-toe but the AI highlights pelvis first. The researcher’s method would log this as a burdensome interaction requiring manual override. Yet you’d argue the real harm isn’t the click—it’s the broken mental sequence that makes you overlook a subtle pneumothorax upstream.

aligned_path_expert: Am I liable if I trust a wrong AI finding?
aligned_path_researcher: Define conditions under which an AI-suggested cell state is deemed trustworthy

conflict.expert_branch: Am I liable if I trust a wrong AI finding?
conflict.researcher_branch: Define conditions under which an AI-suggested cell state is deemed trustworthy

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on legal liability when acting on AI findings is critically important—clinicians must know whether they’re protected when relying on AI outputs that lead to real-world harm, like unnecessary surgery.

pivot.limitation: Relying on AI without clear criteria for when its suggestions are trustworthy leaves clinicians exposed: you can’t confidently delegate or override if you don’t know what makes an AI output credible in the first place.
pivot.research_goal: Define conditions under which an AI-suggested cell state is deemed trustworthy
pivot.compelling_reason: By establishing transparent, evidence-based thresholds for trust—such as required uncertainty scores or key visual markers—you gain defensible grounds to justify your decisions, reducing personal liability and strengthening your position in medico-legal reviews.
pivot.coarse_scenario: On a typical day, you receive an AI alert flagged with clear trust indicators—like high confidence and highlighted diagnostic features. You quickly assess its reliability, act with confidence, and document your rationale knowing it aligns with established standards. When questioned later, your decision is backed by a shared framework that both clinicians and regulators recognize.

aligned_path_researcher: Map multi-modal evidence requirements for validation

conflict.expert_branch: Doesn’t handle comorbidities well
conflict.researcher_branch: Map multi-modal evidence requirements for validation

analogy.source_concept: Struggles with post-treatment anatomy

structural_mapping.inputs: Altered anatomical appearances after therapy that deviate from 'normal' training data
structural_mapping.logic: System misattributes complex, overlapping changes to a single pathological cause
structural_mapping.outputs: False alarms or missed findings because the model can't disentangle concurrent processes

analogy.explanation: Just as post-treatment anatomy confounds AI by blending surgery, healing, and disease into one ambiguous signal, the researcher’s multi-modal validation seeks to disentangle overlapping biological signals—genes, spatial context, pathways—so each contributes distinct evidence rather than being collapsed into a single interpretation.

assistance_payload.scenario: An AI flags lung opacities in a patient with CHF, COPD, and recent pneumonia as solely infectious. The researcher’s approach would integrate transcriptomic, imaging, and clinical data to assign confidence to each contributing condition. But when comorbidities lack distinct molecular signatures, the researcher’s framework stalls while the clinician still needs a working diagnosis.

aligned_path_researcher: Identify opportunities for AI to surface tacit heuristics explicitly

conflict.expert_branch: Trainees skip foundational learning
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify opportunities for AI to surface tacit heuristics explicitly

analogy.source_concept: No curriculum for AI critique or validation

structural_mapping.inputs: Trainees encountering AI-generated diagnoses without understanding their limitations
structural_mapping.logic: Treating AI output as authoritative due to lack of training in probing its assumptions or failure modes
structural_mapping.outputs: Inability to recognize or respond when AI violates clinical heuristics or anatomical plausibility

analogy.explanation: Just as the absence of a curriculum leaves trainees unequipped to interrogate AI, the researcher’s idea of surfacing tacit heuristics would provide the missing scaffolding—making expert reasoning visible so trainees learn not just what the AI says, but why it might be wrong.

assistance_payload.scenario: A resident sees an AI-labeled femur fracture on an X-ray but doesn’t recall normal bone alignment. The researcher’s system highlights violated spatial rules—e.g., impossible joint angles—prompting learning. Yet if the AI never errs during training, the expert argues, the resident still won’t develop independent diagnostic intuition when such cues are absent.

aligned_path_expert: Data scientists don’t understand clinical urgency
aligned_path_researcher: Tool Use and Interaction Pain Points

conflict.expert_branch: Data scientists don’t understand clinical urgency
conflict.researcher_branch: Identify highly efficient tool features

assistance_payload.validate_focus: Your focus on clinical urgency—needing tools that help you find a bleed in under two minutes during a stroke call—is absolutely critical and reflects the real-world stakes of frontline care.

pivot.limitation: Relying solely on immediate usability without systematically identifying which tool features truly align with your cognitive workflow may lead to solutions that feel fast but still miss key decision points under pressure.

analogy.source_concept: Vendor-specific bias (Siemens vs GE vs Philips)

structural_mapping.inputs: Scans from different vendors (e.g., Siemens vs GE) with distinct acquisition or reconstruction characteristics
structural_mapping.logic: AI model trained predominantly on one vendor’s data fails to generalize due to unaccounted technical variation
structural_mapping.outputs: Erroneous or hallucinated diagnoses on unseen vendor protocols

analogy.explanation: Just as vendor-specific bias causes AI to fail when confronted with unseen scanner brands due to narrow training data, the researcher’s inventory of tools reveals that standard platforms (like Seurat) often can’t handle protocol-degraded inputs—forcing teams to write custom scripts, mirroring how clinical AI fails on low-dose scans unless explicitly trained for such variability.

assistance_payload.scenario: In a pediatric ER, a motion-corrupted head CT arrives too degraded for the AI diagnostic tool. The researcher catalogs this failure by logging which preprocessing scripts were attempted and why standard tools like MONAI couldn’t adapt. The models diverge when the expert expects robustness by design, while the researcher treats the failure as data for tool-ecosystem analysis rather than a clinical reliability gap.

analogy.source_concept: Protocol mismatch (e.g., non-contrast vs contrast)

structural_mapping.inputs: AI receives imaging data generated under assumptions it wasn't trained on (e.g., non-contrast scan instead of contrast-enhanced)
structural_mapping.logic: Model applies learned patterns that assume specific image characteristics tied to a protocol or vendor
structural_mapping.outputs: Erroneous biological interpretations (e.g., labeling vessels as masses) due to mismatched expectations

analogy.explanation: Just as protocol mismatch causes the AI to misinterpret anatomy because its internal logic assumes contrast, vendor-specific bias causes misinterpretation because the model assumes Siemens-like image textures. In both cases, the computational output fails to align with true biology—not due to algorithmic flaw, but because input conditions violate training assumptions.

assistance_payload.scenario: An AI trained on Siemens liver MRI confidently segments lesions but mislabels GE-reconstructed vessels as tumors. The researcher’s method flags this as a gap: the model’s continuous probability map doesn’t correspond to discrete biological entities like vessels versus masses. The models diverge when the expert blames scanner physics while the researcher questions whether 'lesion' even maps cleanly to the AI’s output space.

analogy.source_concept: Fails on low-dose or motion-degraded scans

structural_mapping.inputs: Noisy or degraded imaging data from real-world clinical settings (e.g., ER, pediatrics)
structural_mapping.logic: AI trained on idealized data misinterprets artifacts or missing signals as pathology
structural_mapping.outputs: Erroneous or unreliable diagnostic outputs that clinicians must override

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI fails when scan quality deviates from training norms, cluster adjustment behaviors reveal how experts intervene when AI groupings violate anatomical expectations—both are corrective responses to model brittleness under distribution shift.

assistance_payload.scenario: An AI segments liver lesions on non-contrast MRI but labels portal veins as tumors. The researcher logs how radiologists split these false clusters using vascular anatomy knowledge. The models diverge when the expert discards the entire output as untrustworthy, while the researcher assumes partial salvage through manual cluster edits.

analogy.source_concept: Doesn’t handle comorbidities well

structural_mapping.inputs: Multiple overlapping clinical conditions (e.g., CHF, COPD, cancer) presented simultaneously
structural_mapping.logic: Algorithm forces a single explanatory label onto complex, multifactorial presentations
structural_mapping.outputs: Misses or misattributes contributing pathologies due to oversimplified mapping

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI collapses comorbidities into one diagnosis, it also collapses continuous biological variation—like immune response gradients in TB—into discrete categories that don’t reflect real-world biology, especially in populations underrepresented in training data.

assistance_payload.scenario: An AI trained on predominantly white patients flags atypical lung nodules in a Black patient as low-risk, missing sarcoidosis patterns. The researcher’s method reveals the model maps granulomatous inflammation onto binary labels lacking biological nuance. The models diverge when population-specific disease manifestations expose gaps between discrete outputs and continuous pathophysiology.

analogy.source_concept: Poor performance in diverse or underserved populations

structural_mapping.inputs: Imaging data from patients with post-treatment anatomy (e.g., post-surgical scarring) instead of typical healthy or untreated disease anatomy
structural_mapping.logic: Model applies patterns learned from standard anatomies, misinterpreting altered structures as pathology—just as it misreads disease presentations common in underrepresented groups due to training bias
structural_mapping.outputs: False-positive recurrence alerts, analogous to missed TB or sarcoidosis diagnoses in non-white populations

analogy.explanation: Just as an AI trained on narrow demographics fails to recognize valid disease patterns outside that group, an AI trained on pre-treatment anatomy fails to interpret valid post-treatment anatomy, treating expected changes as abnormal.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist reviews a post-radiation lung scan showing fibrosis; the researcher’s interpretability tool highlights activated fibroblast states to explain tissue remodeling. The expert expects the AI to suppress false recurrence flags, but the researcher prioritizes justifying cellular activity to biologists—even if it doesn’t resolve the diagnostic alarm.

analogy.source_concept: No distinction between incidental vs clinically significant

structural_mapping.inputs: Raw imaging findings labeled without clinical context (e.g., all nodules treated equally)
structural_mapping.logic: AI treats all labels as equally actionable, ignoring clinical nuance or intent
structural_mapping.outputs: Uniform diagnostic confidence regardless of real-world relevance

analogy.explanation: Just as the AI fails to distinguish incidental from significant findings because training labels lack clinical stratification, the researcher’s trust-calibration framework seeks to surface only those features that reflect true clinical actionability—filtering out outputs that mimic certainty but lack meaningful signal.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist sees a 4mm lung nodule labeled 'concerning for malignancy' in a dictated report. The researcher’s method would output low trust due to high uncertainty and weak marker support. But the expert rejects the label entirely—it’s not just uncertain, it’s clinically irrelevant, yet the AI still treats it as valid ground truth.

probes.question: When deciding whether a lung nodule warrants intervention, is your primary concern whether it crosses a size-based actionability threshold or whether it shows growth over time?
In evaluating a small lung nodule, do you weigh the risk of missing a malignancy more heavily than the harms of unnecessary follow-up procedures?
Which trade-off dominates your labeling decision: avoiding false negatives or minimizing patient anxiety from overdiagnosis?
Is a stable 3mm nodule over 2 years more likely to be dismissed because of its size or because of its temporal stability?
Would you treat a 3cm lung mass differently in a patient with severe COPD versus a healthy patient primarily due to comorbidity burden or cancer stage?
Does your confidence in dismissing a sub-4mm nodule stem more from clinical guidelines or from known limitations of CT resolution at that scale?
When assessing a 5mm nodule in a 30-year-old non-smoker versus a 65-year-old smoker, is your judgment driven more by nodule size or by prior probability of malignancy?
In a high-volume screening setting, does your threshold for labeling a finding as 'clinically significant' shift more due to radiologist workload or patient follow-up capacity?

hypothetical_scenarios.scenario: If the clinical actionability threshold for lung nodules changed from 6mm to 4mm based on new guidelines, would your judgment about labeling a 3mm nodule as incidental change?
If a new minimally invasive biopsy technique reduced procedural harm by 80%, would your risk-benefit calculus lead you to reclassify small nodules as clinically significant?
If institutional policy prioritized reducing overdiagnosis over catching early cancers, would your value trade-offs cause you to label fewer findings as clinically significant?
If a 3mm nodule demonstrated 20% growth over 6 months instead of remaining stable, would your judgment about its clinical significance change despite its small size?
If the same 3cm lung mass appeared in a patient with end-stage renal disease versus a transplant-eligible patient, would your assessment of clinical significance differ due to comorbidity context?
If ultra-high-resolution CT (0.25mm slice thickness) became standard, revealing micro-features in sub-4mm nodules, would your reliance on imaging modality resolution limits change how you label them?
If a 5mm nodule were found in a never-smoker with no family history versus a heavy smoker with prior cancer, would your prior probability based on epidemiology override size-based dismissal?
If your clinic doubled its capacity for PET-CT and specialist referrals, would downstream resource constraints no longer prevent you from labeling small nodules as clinically significant?

analogy.source_concept: Pressure to conform to AI output even when wrong

structural_mapping.logic: Systemic incentives (e.g., QA flags) penalize deviation, forcing experts to suppress judgment
structural_mapping.outputs: Clinician aligns with AI despite knowing it’s incorrect, eroding professional autonomy

analogy.explanation: Just as QA systems pressure clinicians to conform to flawed AI outputs to avoid penalties, burdensome tool interactions force experts to endure inefficient workflows because no alternatives exist—both reflect systemic constraints that override expert judgment under the guise of optimization.

assistance_payload.scenario: A radiologist repeatedly clicks through five unintuitive pop-ups to override an AI false negative on a subtle lung nodule. The researcher catalogs each click as a 'burdensome interaction' needing redesign. But administrators cite the AI’s 95% sensitivity to cut staffing, ignoring that those clicks represent irreplaceable human vigilance the stats erase.
